item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked mainly for the unusual method of containment. A page that's only one thirds full, having +65 upvotes, makes you pretty curious too. +1

item.title: Re: Yeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeah!
item.content:encoded: In this context, it's so ironic that the hook of the chorus to that is "We won't get fooled again"…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Names having power is one of the most ancient common supernatural elements. Yes, in real life, names are arbitrary designators, but that's the point. The real anomaly in this SCP is the fact that it breaks the ordinary rule "the designation is not something inherited in the object".

item.title: Yeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeah!
item.content:encoded:
Not me, I love infohazards. I love them so much I

*sunglasses*

coined the term.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just so; the names are arbitrary anyhow and shouldn't initiate the effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, I don't know it's name. I don't even know that the coin exists. All I know is that there is this cube, and it's an infohazard. The report doesn't say what it does, and I don't think about it too much because infohazards are nasty. For all I know it creates a chicken every time someone thinks that it's a sphere.

To clarify: SCP-1663 is the cube; the coin doesn't have a designation, because nobody knows that the coin exists.

Also, infohazards that are activated only by their SCP designation stretch my suspension of disbelief somewhat. The designation is not something inherited in the object, it's just a word we use to refer to it. Imagine some poor researcher thinking "Oh, I worked with SCP-2040 last month, and I've read about SCP-2042. I wonder, what SCP-2041 is" and then his heads explodes because 2041 happened to be an infohazard. Most of our existing infohazards wouldn't work if you only know their number: 096 needs you to see his face, 964 can only change what it is, not what other people call it, and 1055 only reacts to fear.

(The fact that your fear 1055 once you know how it works is an example of a non-anomalous memetic effect, by the way. If you are unfamiliar with the concepts of death, pain and danger, then you wouldn't fear him and trigger his growth. But I digress.)

In the case of 1663 it's ambiguous whether knowing the sequence "SCP-Ksi-Lambda-0" triggers the effect, but if SCP-1663 is designation of the cube and the cube only, then keeping the coin in the cube should be reasonably safe either way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ehhh…I've really just grown past the whole contagious infohazard containment switcharoo thingy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is basically the exact same reason I upvoted. It's so viral, the containment has to be contained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Description: SCP-1663 is a 0.1 m x 0.1 m x 0.1 m concrete cube.

From what I got out of it, that still wouldn't work. It's even knowing the current identification of it that would trigger; if you were to walk up to a random, rural Chinese villager and say "This coin is worth trei deustche mark" and the guy would smile, nod… And within a couple of weeks be burning down the whole village out of revulsion. The same thing here, "SCP-1663 is a block of cement" would be just as effective as "SCP-1663 is a coin that…" because you know its NAME. Hence, the unconventional name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The effect is 'meh', but by the time I finished reading I was upvoting. It's not the effect that's interesting here, it's the effects of the effect and how the Foundation has dealt with it. Very nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See, this spawned from a conversation about an infohazard (specifically SCP-1231) being exposed to the O5 Council. So think about what would happen if this was given to the O5s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like what this is going for, and the execution's good, but honestly I was kind of let down by the object's actual effects. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel just a tad let down at the actual effects (and will point out that anyone affected to such extremes would die of dehydration before they ever starved to death), but the containment procedures really build up nicely and paint a good story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My only qualm is how the additional page isn't put under a read more collapsible, but I can understand the intention behind it. +1 because I love me a good infohazard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now this is a wealth-based coin SCP done right. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Description: SCP-1663 is a 0.1 m x 0.1 m x 0.1 m concrete cube.

Or something like that. Assigned personnel wouldn't even know that the coin exists.

I am no-voting on the main article, because the current containment would work, it's just that it seems unnecessary complicated to me.

Still, this seems… incomplete, somehow. The main page is essentially just the containment procedures, with all the description left for the supplementary one. And while the procedures are moderately clever, the object itself is just a generic boring thing.

Maybe I just have a higher than normal quality bar for infohazards because they are so much fun and I love them so much, I don't know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unambiguously identifying SCP-Ksi-Lambda-0 triggers its effect. That's the entire point. That's why the SCP is named strangely, 1663 is ONLY the site that it's contained at.

Edit: I will clarify this on the supplementary page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, well done Raven. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aahahaha, oops.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is way too extreme a containment. Since knowing that 1663 exist without knowing what it is doesn't produce the effect (and it doesn't, or else everyone who would read the main page would be affected), containment procedures similar to those of SCP-370 should work reasonably well.

I also dislike how it's handled out-of-character; I would've preferred the current main page to be a splash page, and the current supplemental page to be the main page.

Also, the effect itself is boring as atheist's hell.

And why would they assign the same number to the site and to the object, in-character? Clearly there are people who are authorized to know about site's existence without being authorized to know what is actually kept there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hm. The effect itself is kind of bland, and dangerous enough that I'm not sure what research could be conducted. This seems like something you just lock up and forget about, because that's the best way to contain it. Also, this line:

To date, ████ (5) retiring O5 council members

You might want to rephrase. There's knowing what's behind a piece of redaction, and then there's this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And this SCP handles it very well. Upvoted hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Props to Reject for giving me the inspiration for this SCP. We had a conversation that resulted in a what-if scenario regarding an extreme infohazard coupled with the Foundation's standing policy to not allow O5 Council members to be exposed to SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice. I think you have handled this sort of infohazard's containment procedures better than Eskobar with the Catcher in the Rye book.
